script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Thursday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed four point five six percent down on the session.

Taiwan Semiconductor reported strong topline numbers, then dropped a capital expenditure reset that rattled the entire semiconductor complex. That spending revision hit free cash flow projections across the board, and the selling was immediate and broad.

The broader market closed sharply lower Thursday. The Nasdaq led declines as artificial intelligence and semiconductor names absorbed the heaviest losses, extending a selloff that began Wednesday following earnings commentary from ASML. No major macro data points were available to offset the sector pressure, leaving chip stocks exposed to pure sentiment-driven selling.

Taiwan Semiconductor's earnings report was the fulcrum for Thursday's session. The company delivered topline strength — revenue growth that confirmed continued demand for advanced node manufacturing — but paired that with a capital expenditure reset that immediately compressed free cash flow expectations. For investors who had priced semiconductor equipment and memory names on the assumption that the artificial intelligence infrastructure buildout would generate clean margin expansion, that spending revision introduced a critical question: how much of the AI capital cycle benefits chip companies versus consumes them? The ripple was swift. Memory names, equipment makers, and logic chip designers all sold off in sequence. According to reporting from multiple financial outlets, the move compounded a sector-wide selloff that had already started with ASML's commentary the prior session. For this portfolio, which holds concentrated positions across exactly that supply chain, the damage was uniform and significant.

Advanced Micro Devices was down five point three three percent on the session. A broader article from Yahoo Finance flagged Advanced Micro Devices alongside Intel and Micron as names where Wall Street is actively questioning how long the artificial intelligence spending boom can sustain current valuations. The stock's Q1 earnings showed competitive positioning in processors and graphics chips, but the near-term overhang is macro, not company-specific. Watch for any commentary from major cloud hyperscalers about data center budget revisions.

Micron Technology was down five point six five percent. Multiple outlets identified Micron as a central name in Thursday's artificial intelligence memory selloff. One report noted that a single company currently supplies fifty-six percent of the world's artificial intelligence memory chips — a figure that underscores both the opportunity and the concentration risk. Micron's exposure to that market makes it a direct proxy for AI infrastructure sentiment. Watch HBM pricing signals closely.

NVIDIA was down two point four zero percent — notably the most resilient of the large-cap artificial intelligence names in this portfolio. No company-specific news drove the move. The decline appears to reflect sector contagion rather than any fundamental shift. Relative outperformance versus peers on a down day is worth noting.

Broadcom was down five point zero three percent on the session. No stock-specific catalyst was identified. The selloff tracks the broader equipment and connectivity infrastructure concern around capital expenditure compression following Taiwan Semiconductor's report. Custom silicon demand from hyperscalers remains the key variable to monitor.

Intel was down five point eight four percent — the steepest single-session decline among the primary holdings Thursday. No company-specific news was flagged. Intel remains the most operationally challenged name in this portfolio, and in a risk-off semiconductor session, it attracted disproportionate selling pressure. Execution on its foundry roadmap remains the long-term watch item.

Applied Materials was down three point one nine percent on the session. As a semiconductor equipment supplier, Applied Materials sits directly in the path of any capital expenditure revision narrative. If manufacturers are resetting spending plans, equipment order pipelines face scrutiny. Next earnings commentary will be critical.

KLA Corporation was down two point two nine percent — the second-most resilient name in the portfolio Thursday behind NVIDIA. No specific news was attributed to KLA. Its relative strength in a hard session for equipment names suggests some differentiation at the stock level. Inspection and process control demand remains structurally supported.

Marvell Technology was down eight point seven one percent — the single worst performer in the portfolio Thursday by a significant margin. No company-specific news was pinpointed as the direct cause, but Marvell's deep exposure to custom artificial intelligence networking silicon makes it highly sensitive to any demand uncertainty narrative around data center buildout. This is the position that most warrants monitoring into next week.

Lam Research was down four point three one percent on the session. As a deposition and etch equipment supplier, Lam Research faces the same capital expenditure revision headwind as Applied Materials. No stock-specific news emerged. Relative performance was roughly in line with the equipment peer group.

Taiwan Semiconductor was down two point three two percent. As the source of Thursday's catalyst, its own stock move was comparatively contained. The market appears to have differentiated between the messenger and the downstream exposed names. Upcoming guidance revisions and any customer commentary on wafer starts will set the tone going forward.

Three data points to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, any hyperscaler capital expenditure commentary — Microsoft, Alphabet, or Amazon guidance updates on data center spending directly affect demand visibility for this entire portfolio. Second, watch SK Hynix's next public statement on high-bandwidth memory pricing after Thursday's sharp declines in memory names. Third, monitor ASML's full earnings call transcript for any order book revision language that could extend the equipment sector selloff into Friday.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch whether the Taiwan Semiconductor-driven selling pressure finds a floor, or whether Friday brings a second consecutive broad semiconductor down session.
